lessons.title: What a Force Is, and Newton's First Law
lessons.content:
Why do things start moving, stop, speed up, or change direction? The answer is almost always a **force**. Forces are everywhere: when you open a door, kick a ball, or sit in a chair, forces are at work.

**Build:** A force is simply a **push or a pull** on an object. That's it. Kicking a ball is a push; tugging a rope is a pull. Forces have a **size** (how strong) and a **direction** (which way). We measure the size in a unit called the **newton** (symbol **N**), named after the scientist Isaac Newton. As a feel for it, holding a small apple takes about 1 N.

Now the big idea, **Newton's First Law** (also called the law of **inertia**): an object will keep doing what it is already doing unless a force changes it. A still object stays still; a moving object keeps moving in a straight line at a steady speed, *until a force acts on it*. **Inertia** is just this tendency of objects to resist changes in their motion. Heavier objects have more inertia.

A common trap: people think motion itself needs a constant force to keep going. It does not. A puck slides forever on perfect frictionless ice with no push at all. On real ice it slows only because **friction** (a force) acts on it. No force needed to *keep* moving, only to *change* the motion.

**Recap:** A force is a push or pull measured in newtons. With no net force, an object keeps its current motion. That is Newton's First Law, the law of inertia.

items.prompt: In physics, what exactly is a force?
choices: A push or a pull on an object
Only the speed of an object
The weight of an object alone
A type of energy stored in motion
items.explanation: A force is simply a push or a pull. Speed and weight are related ideas but are not the definition of force. Energy is a separate concept.

items.prompt: What is the unit used to measure the size of a force?
choices: The newton (N)
The kilogram (kg)
The meter (m)
The second (s)
items.explanation: Force is measured in newtons (N). The kilogram measures mass, the meter measures distance, and the second measures time.

items.prompt: A hockey puck slides across perfectly frictionless ice at a steady speed in a straight line. What force is needed to KEEP it moving like that?
choices: No force at all
A constant forward push
A force equal to its weight
A pull in the direction it moves
items.explanation: This targets the classic misconception that motion needs a force to continue. By Newton's First Law, with no net force an object keeps moving in a straight line at steady speed. A force is only needed to CHANGE the motion.

items.prompt: What does 'inertia' mean?
choices: An object's tendency to resist changes in its motion
The force of gravity pulling things down
The speed an object is moving at
The amount of friction on a surface
items.explanation: Inertia is the resistance of an object to changes in its motion. It is not a force itself and not the same as speed or gravity.

items.prompt: Which object has the MOST inertia?
choices: A loaded freight truck
A bicycle
A tennis ball
A sheet of paper
items.explanation: More mass means more inertia, so the heavy freight truck resists changes in motion the most. That is why a truck is so hard to start moving and so hard to stop.

items.prompt: A book rests still on a table and stays still. What does Newton's First Law say about this?
choices: It stays still because no net force is acting to change its motion
It stays still because objects naturally prefer to be at rest
It stays still because gravity has been turned off
It stays still only because the table is heavier than the book
items.explanation: An object at rest stays at rest when there is no net (overall) force changing its motion. Gravity still pulls down, but the table pushes up equally, so the forces cancel.

items.prompt: Roughly how big is a force of 1 newton in everyday terms?
choices: About the weight of a small apple held in your hand
About the weight of a car
About the push needed to move a mountain
Too tiny for any object to feel
items.explanation: One newton is roughly the weight of a small apple. This everyday feel helps you judge whether a force value is small or large.

items.prompt: A car cruising on a highway suddenly slows down. According to Newton's First Law, what must be true?
choices: Some force (like braking or friction) acted on it
It ran out of inertia
No explanation is needed, since moving things naturally slow
Its mass decreased
items.explanation: A change in motion always means a net force acted, here the brakes and friction. Objects do not 'run out of inertia,' and motion does not naturally fade without a force.

items.prompt: Two forces act on a box: 5 N pushing right and 5 N pushing left. What is the net (overall) force, and what happens?
choices: 0 N net; the box's motion does not change
10 N net to the right; the box speeds up
5 N net; the box drifts left
The box must fall down
items.explanation: Equal and opposite forces cancel, giving zero net force. With no net force, motion does not change, exactly what the First Law predicts.

lessons.title: Newton's Second Law (F = m a), Mass vs Weight
lessons.content:
The First Law tells us a force *changes* motion. Newton's **Second Law** tells us *how much*. This is the workhorse equation of physics, and it explains why a light cart is easy to push but a loaded one is not.

**Build:** When a net force acts on an object, the object **accelerates**, meaning its motion changes (it speeds up, slows down, or turns). The rule is:

$$F = m \\times a$$

In words: net **force** (F, in newtons) equals **mass** (m, in kilograms) times **acceleration** (a, in meters per second squared, written $\\text{m/s}^2$). Two patterns fall out. For the same object, a **bigger push gives bigger acceleration**. For the same push, a **heavier object accelerates less**, because mass resists changes in motion.

Now a crucial distinction. **Mass** is the amount of 'stuff' in an object, measured in kilograms; it never changes no matter where you are. **Weight** is the *force* of gravity pulling on that mass, measured in newtons, and it changes with location. On Earth, weight = mass times $g$, where $g \\approx 9.8\\,\\text{m/s}^2$. So a 10 kg bag has a weight of about 98 N on Earth.

A common mistake: saying something 'weighs 10 kg.' Kilograms measure mass, not weight. On the Moon that same 10 kg of stuff is unchanged, but it *weighs* about one sixth as much because the Moon's gravity is weaker.

**Recap:** $F = m a$ links force, mass, and acceleration. Mass (kg) is constant; weight (N) is the pull of gravity and depends on where you are.

items.prompt: What does Newton's Second Law, F = m a, relate?
choices: Net force, mass, and acceleration
Weight, height, and time
Speed, distance, and friction
Push, pull, and color
items.explanation: F = m a connects the net force on an object to its mass and its acceleration. The other options mix in unrelated quantities.

items.prompt: You push two boxes with the same force, but box B has twice the mass of box A. How do their accelerations compare?
choices: Box A accelerates twice as much as box B
They accelerate the same
Box B accelerates twice as much as box A
Box B does not move at all
items.explanation: From a = F / m, more mass means less acceleration for the same force. Doubling the mass halves the acceleration, so the lighter box A accelerates twice as much.

items.prompt: A net force of 10 N acts on a 2 kg cart. What is its acceleration?
items.explanation: Rearrange F = m a to a = F / m = 10 N / 2 kg = 5 m/s^2. Multiplying instead of dividing (giving 20) is the common slip.

items.prompt: What is the difference between mass and weight?
choices: Mass is the amount of stuff (kg); weight is the force of gravity on it (N)
They are exactly the same thing
Mass is measured in newtons; weight in kilograms
Weight never changes but mass does
items.explanation: Mass measures how much matter is in an object (kilograms) and stays constant. Weight is the gravitational force on that mass (newtons) and changes with gravity.

items.prompt: An astronaut takes a 10 kg toolbox to the Moon. What happens to its mass and weight?
choices: Mass stays 10 kg; weight becomes smaller
Both mass and weight become smaller
Both stay exactly the same
Mass becomes smaller; weight stays the same
items.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter and does not change with location, so it stays 10 kg. Weight is gravity's pull, and the Moon's weaker gravity makes it weigh about one sixth as much.

items.prompt: On Earth (g approximately 9.8 m/s^2), what is the approximate weight of a 5 kg object?
choices: About 49 N
About 5 N
About 9.8 N
About 0.5 N
items.explanation: Weight = mass x g = 5 kg x 9.8 m/s^2, which is about 49 N. Reporting 5 confuses mass (kg) with weight (N).

items.prompt: Why is it not quite right to say a bag of flour 'weighs 2 kilograms'?
choices: Kilograms measure mass, not weight; weight is a force in newtons
Because flour has no weight at all
Because only liquids can be weighed
Because kilograms only apply on the Moon
items.explanation: Strictly, kilograms measure mass. The bag's weight (the force of gravity on it) would be in newtons. Everyday speech blurs this, but physics keeps them separate.

items.prompt: You double the net force on a cart while keeping its mass the same. What happens to its acceleration?
choices: It doubles
It stays the same
It is cut in half
It becomes zero
items.explanation: Since a = F / m, doubling F with m unchanged doubles a. Force and acceleration are directly proportional for a fixed mass.

items.prompt: A 4 kg object accelerates at 3 m/s^2. What net force is acting on it?
choices: 7 N
items.explanation: Use F = m a = 4 kg x 3 m/s^2 = 12 N. Adding the numbers (7) or dividing them are the typical errors.

lessons.title: Newton's Third Law: Action and Reaction
lessons.content:
Have you ever pushed off a wall while on roller skates and rolled backward? You pushed the wall, yet *you* moved. Newton's **Third Law** explains this, and it is the most misunderstood of the three.

**Build:** The law states: for every action, there is an **equal and opposite reaction**. More precisely, whenever object A pushes or pulls object B, object B pushes or pulls back on A with a force that is **equal in size** and **opposite in direction**. Forces always come in **pairs**.

The key, often-missed point: the two forces in a pair act on **different objects**. When your foot pushes down on the ground, the ground pushes up on your foot. That upward push on you is what lets you walk and jump. A swimmer pushes water backward; the water pushes the swimmer forward. A rocket pushes gas out the back; the gas pushes the rocket forward.

Now the big misconception: people think the action-reaction pair cancels out, so nothing should ever move. They do **not** cancel, because they act on **two different objects**. Forces only cancel when they act on the *same* object. The ground's push on your foot and your foot's push on the ground act on different things, so each can cause its own motion.

Another trap: in a tug-of-war or a collision, both objects feel the *same size* force even if one is much heavier. The heavier one just accelerates less (remember $a = F/m$).

**Recap:** Forces come in equal, opposite pairs acting on two different objects. Because they act on different objects, they do not cancel.

items.prompt: Newton's Third Law says that for every action force there is...
choices: An equal and opposite reaction force on a different object
A larger reaction force in the same direction
No reaction at all
A reaction only if the object is heavy
items.explanation: Every force comes with a partner that is equal in size, opposite in direction, and acts on the OTHER object involved.

items.prompt: Your foot pushes down on the ground with 200 N. What is the reaction force?
choices: The ground pushes up on your foot with 200 N
Your foot pushes up on itself with 200 N
The ground pushes down with 200 N
There is no reaction because the ground cannot move
items.explanation: The reaction is equal (200 N), opposite (upward), and acts on the other object (your foot). This upward push is what lets you walk and jump.

items.prompt: If action and reaction forces are equal and opposite, why don't they always cancel and stop everything from moving?
choices: Because they act on two different objects, not the same one
Because one force is secretly larger
Because reaction forces are imaginary
Because they act at different times
items.explanation: This is the central misconception. Forces only cancel when they act on the SAME object. Action-reaction pairs act on different objects, so each can cause motion.

items.prompt: A swimmer moves forward through water. What is the action-reaction pair?
choices: Swimmer pushes water backward; water pushes swimmer forward
Swimmer pushes water forward; water pushes swimmer backward
Gravity pulls down; the swimmer floats up
The swimmer pushes the air, which pushes the water
items.explanation: The swimmer's hands push water backward, and by the Third Law the water pushes the swimmer forward. That forward push is what propels the swimmer.

items.prompt: A large truck collides head-on with a small car. How do the forces they exert on each other compare?
choices: They feel equal-size forces on each other
The truck feels a much larger force
The car feels a much larger force
Only the car feels any force
items.explanation: By the Third Law, the two contact forces are always equal in size. The car is damaged more because its smaller mass means a much larger acceleration (a = F / m), not a larger force.

items.prompt: How does a rocket in empty space move forward, with nothing to push against?
choices: It pushes gas out the back; the gas pushes the rocket forward
It pushes against the air around it
It pulls itself along using gravity
It cannot move in empty space
items.explanation: The rocket pushes exhaust gas backward, and the gas pushes the rocket forward, an action-reaction pair. No outside air or surface is needed, which is why rockets work in space.

items.prompt: Which is a TRUE action-reaction pair (Third Law pair)?
choices: Earth pulls the apple down; the apple pulls Earth up
Gravity pulls the apple down; the table pushes the apple up
The apple's weight; the apple's mass
The apple's speed; the apple's direction
items.explanation: A true pair is the same interaction seen from both sides: Earth pulls the apple, so the apple pulls Earth equally. The table-and-gravity pair acts on the SAME object (the apple), so it is not a Third Law pair.

items.prompt: Two skaters of different sizes push off each other on frictionless ice. The forces are equal. Who speeds away faster?
choices: The lighter skater
The heavier skater
They move at the same speed
Neither moves
items.explanation: Equal forces, but a = F / m, so the lighter skater (smaller m) gets the larger acceleration and moves away faster. Equal force does not mean equal motion.

items.prompt: A horse pulls a cart. Some say 'the cart pulls back equally, so they can never move.' What is the flaw?
choices: The two forces act on different objects, and other forces (ground on hooves) move the system
The horse actually pulls harder than the cart
Reaction forces do not really exist
The cart has no mass
items.explanation: The horse-cart forces act on different objects, so they don't cancel each other. The horse also pushes the ground, and the ground pushes the horse forward, allowing the whole system to accelerate.

lessons.title: Common Forces and Free-Body Diagrams
lessons.content:
To predict how an object moves, you first need to list every force acting on it. A simple sketch called a **free-body diagram** does exactly that, and it is the single most useful problem-solving tool in mechanics.

**Build:** A free-body diagram shows just **one object** (drawn as a box or dot) with an **arrow for every force** acting on it. Each arrow points in the force's direction; longer arrows mean larger forces. Four everyday forces show up again and again:

- **Weight** ($W$): the pull of gravity, always pointing **straight down**, equal to $m g$.
- **Normal force** ($N$): the push a surface gives **perpendicular** (at a right angle) to itself; for a flat floor this points **up**. 'Normal' here is the math word for 'at a right angle,' not 'ordinary.'
- **Friction** ($f$): a force from surfaces in contact that **opposes sliding**, pointing **opposite** to the motion (or attempted motion).
- **Tension** ($T$): the pull transmitted **through a rope, string, or cable**, directed **along the rope**, away from the object.

To use the diagram, add the arrows. If the up and down forces cancel, and the left and right forces cancel, the **net force is zero** and the object's motion does not change (First Law). If they do not cancel, the leftover net force causes acceleration in that direction ($F = m a$).

A common error is forgetting the normal force or drawing weight pointing along a slope; weight is *always* straight down toward Earth's center, no matter how the surface is tilted.

**Recap:** Draw one object, one arrow per force (weight, normal, friction, tension, and any push). Add them up: balanced means no change in motion, unbalanced means acceleration.

items.prompt: What does a free-body diagram show?
choices: One object with an arrow for every force acting on it
The path an object travels over time
Every object in a room and how they connect
Only the motion, with no forces drawn
items.explanation: A free-body diagram isolates a single object and draws one arrow per force acting on it. It is not a map of the path or of other objects.

items.prompt: In which direction does the weight force always point?
choices: Straight down toward Earth
In the direction of motion
Along whatever surface the object rests on
Always upward
items.explanation: Weight is gravity's pull and always points straight down toward Earth's center, regardless of how a surface is tilted. Drawing it along a slope is a common mistake.

items.prompt: What is the 'normal force'?
choices: The push a surface gives perpendicular to itself
The ordinary, everyday amount of force
The force of gravity on an object
The pull of a rope
items.explanation: 'Normal' is the math term for 'perpendicular' (at a right angle). For an object on a flat floor, the normal force points straight up. It is not 'normal' in the everyday sense.

items.prompt: A box is pushed to the right and slides along the floor. Which way does the friction force point?
choices: To the left, opposing the motion
To the right, helping the motion
Straight up
Straight down
items.explanation: Friction opposes sliding, so for a box moving right, friction points left. It always works against the motion (or attempted motion).

items.prompt: A crate hangs at rest from a rope attached to the ceiling. What is the tension force in the rope, and which way does it act on the crate?
choices: A pull along the rope, upward, balancing the crate's weight
A push downward, adding to the weight
Zero, because the crate is not moving
Sideways, perpendicular to the rope
items.explanation: Tension is the pull carried through a rope, directed along the rope away from the object, here upward. Since the crate hangs still, that upward tension balances the downward weight.

items.prompt: On a free-body diagram, the up and down forces cancel and the left and right forces cancel. What can you conclude?
choices: The net force is zero, so the object's motion does not change
The object must be speeding up
The object must be at rest
Gravity has been removed
items.explanation: Balanced forces mean zero net force, so by the First Law the motion does not change. Note the object could be at rest OR moving at steady speed, balanced does not force it to be still.

items.prompt: A box on a flat floor has weight 50 N down and a normal force 50 N up, plus an applied push 8 N right and friction 8 N left. What is the net force?
choices: Zero net force
8 N to the right
50 N downward
16 N to the right
items.explanation: Up and down cancel (50 N each), and left and right cancel (8 N each), so the net force is zero. The box's motion does not change.

items.prompt: A box on a flat floor: weight 30 N down, normal 30 N up, applied push 20 N right, friction 12 N left. What is the net force and resulting motion?
choices: 8 N to the right; the box accelerates to the right
32 N to the right; the box accelerates right
Zero; the box stays still
8 N to the left; the box slows
items.explanation: Vertical forces cancel. Horizontally, 20 N right minus 12 N friction left leaves 8 N right, so the box accelerates rightward (F = m a).

items.prompt: Which is the most common error when drawing a free-body diagram?
choices: Forgetting the normal force, or drawing weight along a slope instead of straight down
Drawing too few objects in the room
Using arrows instead of words
Labeling the units in newtons
items.explanation: Beginners often leave out the normal (surface) force, or tilt the weight arrow to follow a ramp. Weight always points straight down; the normal force should not be forgotten.
